checks.a_analysis: The output document was cross-referenced against the source PDF. No fabricated paragraphs or sentences were identified. All substantive text in the output can be traced back to the source document. The document title is a standard AI-generated placeholder for accessibility, which is an acceptable change.

All contact information (none present), addresses (301 North Wilmington Street), and URLs were checked. While a URL is missing in the output (noted in C5), there are no fabricated URLs or phone numbers that do not exist in the source.
All pages (1-6) and top-level numbered sections (1 through 9) are represented in the output. No entire section or page was silently dropped.
The source document does not contain any tables; therefore, no rows were missing from the output.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: There are multiple significant text omissions. 1) On page 3, a URL (http://www.ncgicc.org/Portals/3/documents/Location_of_County_GIS_Operations.pdf) is completely missing from the end of a paragraph. 2) On page 5, an entire nested list of three bullet points under 'Source data questions:' is omitted. 3) Two section headings ('a. 911/NG911 in the Media' and 'b. AddressNC and Census Geospatial Data') are missing from the sections on pages 1 and 2. These omissions significantly impact the information density of the technical discussions.
checks.b_evidence_source:
1. (http://www.ncgicc.org/Portals/3/documents/Location_of_County_GIS_Operations.pdf). 
2. o Is current satellite imagery (applied in 1996) suitable as the source for classification? o Would color infrared imagery, if funded and processed from statewide imagery acquisition, be the best source for statewide land cover classification? o Would National Agriculture Imagery Program (NAIP) leaf-on imagery be adequate for statewide land cover classification? 
3. a. 911/NG911 in the Media; b. AddressNC and Census Geospatial Data
checks.b_evidence_output:
1. [Text ends after 'departmental locations for GIS'] 
2. [Nested list is null for 'Source data questions:'] 
3. [Headings are absent before the paragraphs in the output]
checks.b_evidence_location: 1. Section 3 (p3), reading_order 19; 2. Section 6 (p5), reading_order 43; 3. Section 3 (p1-2), before reading_order 6 and 8.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: A minor wording change was observed on page 1, where the word 'be' was omitted from a sentence, slightly altering the grammar although the intent remains clear.
checks.b_evidence_source: once the national standard is in place, what would be the utility of a North Carolina specific schema?
checks.b_evidence_output: once the national standard is in place, what would the utility of a North Carolina specific schema?

checks.a_analysis: I have reviewed the output and compared it against the source document. There are no instances of duplicated paragraphs, repeated sections, or content appearing twice where it only exists once in the original PDF. Text blocks follow the source closely without redundancy.

The heading hierarchy is inconsistently applied and contains omissions. Peer-level sections (4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9) are assigned different heading levels in the JSON (4 and 7 are H2, while 5, 6, 8, and 9 are H3). Additionally, the subsection heading 'a. 911/NG911 in the Media' on page 1 is completely absent from the output, which breaks the structural organization of the 'Quick Updates' section.
checks.b_evidence_source:
4. Working Group for Professional Land Surveying and GIS [H2 peer]
5. GICC Website [H2 peer]
6. Committee Reports [H2 peer]
a. 911/NG911 in the Media [Subsection heading]
checks.b_evidence_output:
"text": "4. Working Group for Professional Land Surveying and GIS", "level": 2
"text": "5. GICC Website", "level": 3
"text": "6. Committee Reports", "level": 3
[Heading 'a. 911/NG911 in the Media' is missing between elements 5 and 6]

checks.a_analysis: The source document does not contain any tables; therefore, no table structural corruption is possible.
The reading order of the document follows the logical flow from top to bottom as presented in the source PDF. Major headings and paragraphs are sequenced correctly.

There are two defects in list conversion. First, the main numbered items (1, 2, 3) on page 1 were converted to an unordered list, and their specific numeric prefixes ('1.', '2.', '3.') were stripped from the text. Second, and more critically, the nested sub-bullets under 'Source data questions:' on page 5 are entirely missing from the output. The JSON shows a 'nested_list' value of null for the parent item, resulting in the loss of three distinct data points.
checks.b_evidence_source:
Source data questions:
o Is current satellite imagery...
o Would color infrared imagery...
o Would National Agriculture Imagery Program (NAIP)...

checks.a_analysis: A government URL provided in the text on page 3 is rendered as plain text within a paragraph. It is not identified as a clickable link object in the structured output, failing to preserve the interactive utility of the reference.
checks.b_evidence_output: "text": "A survey in 2010 found a variety of departmental locations for GIS (http://www.ncgicc.org/Portals/3/documents/Location_of_County_GIS_Operations.pdf)."

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output maintains the visual organization of the source PDF. It presents the meeting minutes in a clear, single-column format that matches the original document's flow. Header information (title, date, location) is correctly placed at the top, followed by the numbered minutes. The inclusion of a 'Contents' sidebar is an expected navigational enhancement and does not disrupt the layout fidelity.
The source PDF does not contain any tables. Therefore, there are no tables to be misrendered in the HTML output.
The source PDF does not contain any meaningful images, logos, or charts. The HTML output correctly reflects this absence of graphical content.

There is no evidence of duplicated body content in the HTML output. Each paragraph and section from the source PDF appears exactly once in the document body. The presence of section titles in the sidebar/Table of Contents is a standard accessibility feature and does not constitute content duplication.
